---
title: "Chapter 25 — 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 24 · 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning](ch24.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [26 · ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory →](ch26.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Deep reinforcement learning (deep RL) is what happens when you stop storing a separate value for every state in a giant table and instead let a neural network *generalize* across states. That single substitution — table replaced by function approximator — unlocked agents that play Atari from raw pixels, beat human champions at Go, and align large language models to human preferences. This chapter sits at the top of the Reinforcement Learning part: it assumes you know what an MDP, a value function, and a policy are, and shows how deep networks scale those ideas to problems that classical tabular methods could never touch.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Reinforcement Learning · learning control policies from reward when the state space is too large to enumerate · replace the lookup table with a neural network and fight the instability that creates.
:::

## 25.1 — Why neural networks, and why they break RL

In tabular RL you keep one number per state (or state–action pair): $Q(s,a)$ lives in a literal array, and learning just nudges individual cells. That works until the state space explodes. An Atari frame is $210 \times 160$ pixels — there are more possible screens than atoms in the universe. You will never visit the same exact frame twice, so a table is useless. You need a function that takes a state and *predicts* its value, generalizing from the states you have seen to the ones you have not. A neural network is exactly that function.

So we replace $Q(s,a)$ with $Q_\theta(s,a)$, a network with weights $\theta$, and similarly a policy becomes $\pi_\theta(a\mid s)$. This is **function approximation**: the network shares structure across states, so learning about one frame improves predictions on similar frames. The flip side is that there is no longer a single cell to correct — every weight update touches *every* prediction, so an update meant to fix one state can quietly break a thousand others.

The catch is that RL violates almost every assumption that makes supervised deep learning stable. Three problems stack up:

- **Correlated data.** Consecutive frames are nearly identical, so gradient steps are not on independent samples — the network overfits to whatever it just saw. Supervised learning assumes i.i.d. data; an agent walking through an environment produces the opposite.
- **Non-stationary targets.** In supervised learning the labels are fixed. In RL the "label" for $Q(s,a)$ is built from $Q$ itself (**bootstrapping** — estimating a value from your own later estimates), so the target moves every time you update the weights. You are chasing a target you keep shoving.
- **The deadly triad.** Combine function approximation, bootstrapping, and **off-policy** learning (learning about one policy from data generated by another) and the value estimates can diverge to infinity. Any two of the three are fine; all three together is genuinely dangerous.

Most of the engineering in deep RL is machinery to tame these three. Keep this in mind as a lens: every "trick" below — replay buffers, target networks, clipped objectives, trust regions — is fighting one of these pathologies.

::: {.callout-warning}
Deep RL is not "supervised learning with a reward signal." The targets are non-stationary and the data distribution depends on the policy you are still training. A loss curve going down does not mean the agent is improving — it can mean the network has matched a moving target that is itself wrong.
:::

## 25.2 — Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and the tricks that made it work

The first deep RL system to grab the world's attention was **DQN** (Deep Q-Network, DeepMind 2013/2015), which learned to play dozens of Atari games from pixels using one architecture and one set of hyperparameters. The idea is to approximate the optimal action-value function with a convolutional network $Q_\theta(s,a)$ and train it toward the Bellman optimality target.

Recall the Q-learning update target for a transition $(s,a,r,s')$:

$$y = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$$

We want $Q_\theta(s,a)$ to match $y$, so the loss is just squared error:

$$L(\theta) = \big(r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s', a') - Q_\theta(s,a)\big)^2$$

Naively minimizing this with a neural network diverges. DQN added two tricks that turned it from broken to landmark:

**Experience replay.** Instead of learning from each transition as it arrives, store transitions $(s,a,r,s')$ in a large circular buffer and train on *random minibatches* sampled from it. This breaks the temporal correlation (consecutive frames no longer dominate a batch) and reuses each experience many times, improving sample efficiency.

**Target network.** Notice the $\theta^-$ in the loss above. DQN keeps a *frozen copy* of the network, $Q_{\theta^-}$, used only to compute targets, and refreshes it every few thousand steps. This stops the target from moving on every gradient step — you fix the goalposts for a while, chase them, then move them. Without it, training oscillates or diverges.

```python
# one DQN gradient step (numpy-ish pseudocode)
batch = replay.sample(32)                  # break correlation
q      = Q(batch.s)[batch.a]               # current estimate Q_theta(s,a)
with torch.no_grad():
    q_next = Q_target(batch.s2).max(1)     # frozen target net Q_theta-
    y = batch.r + gamma * q_next * (1 - batch.done)  # no bootstrap past terminal
loss = ((q - y) ** 2).mean()               # regression toward Bellman target
loss.backward(); opt.step()
# every C steps: Q_target.load_state_dict(Q.state_dict())  # refresh goalposts
```

Note the `(1 - done)` mask: at a terminal state there is no future, so the target is just $r$. Forgetting this is one of the most common DQN bugs.

DQN is **off-policy** (it learns the greedy policy while behaving with an exploratory $\epsilon$-greedy one) and works only for **discrete** action spaces, because the $\max_{a'}$ enumerates actions. Many refinements followed — Double DQN (decouple action selection from evaluation to fight overestimation), Dueling DQN (split value and advantage streams), Prioritized Replay (sample surprising transitions more often), and Rainbow (combine them all).

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: if your actions are discrete and you have a cheap simulator you can hammer for millions of steps, a DQN variant is a strong, well-understood baseline. If actions are continuous, skip to the policy-gradient and actor-critic family.
:::

## 25.3 — Policy gradients: optimizing the policy directly

DQN learns values and *derives* a policy by taking the max. **Policy-gradient** methods skip the middleman: parameterize the policy $\pi_\theta(a\mid s)$ directly and adjust $\theta$ to increase expected return. The appeal is that they handle continuous and stochastic actions naturally, and they optimize the thing you actually care about — the policy — rather than a value function you hope implies a good policy.

The objective is expected return $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[R(\tau)]$ over trajectories $\tau$. The problem: the distribution we are averaging over depends on $\theta$, so how do we differentiate through it? The **log-likelihood trick** (a.k.a. the score-function or REINFORCE estimator) is the key identity:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\left[\sum_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, R(\tau)\right]$$

The intuition is beautifully simple: **push up the probability of actions that led to high return, push down those that led to low return**, weighted by how good the outcome was. You do not need to differentiate the environment at all — only the policy's own log-probability, which the network gives you for free.

The basic algorithm is **REINFORCE**: run an episode, compute the return, and take a gradient step proportional to $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t\mid s_t)$ times the return.

```python
# REINFORCE on one episode
logps, rewards = [], []
s = env.reset()
while not done:
    probs = policy(s)                 # pi_theta(.|s)
    a = sample(probs)
    logps.append(log(probs[a]))       # log pi_theta(a|s)
    s, r, done = env.step(a)
    rewards.append(r)

R = discounted_returns(rewards, gamma)        # G_t per step
R = (R - R.mean()) / (R.std() + 1e-8)         # baseline: subtract mean to cut variance
loss = -(stack(logps) * R).sum()              # ascend the log-likelihood-weighted return
loss.backward(); opt.step()
```

The fatal weakness is **variance**. The return $R(\tau)$ is a sum of many noisy rewards, so the gradient estimate is extremely noisy and learning is slow and unstable. The first fix, visible above, is a **baseline**: subtract a state-dependent reference $b(s)$ from the return. As long as the baseline does not depend on the action, it does not bias the gradient but can dramatically cut its variance:

$$\nabla_\theta J = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t\mid s_t)\,\big(R_t - b(s_t)\big)\right]$$

The best baseline is the state's value $V(s)$ — which is exactly what leads us to actor-critic. Policy gradients are also **on-policy**: each gradient estimate is only valid for the policy that generated the data, so once you update $\theta$ you generally must throw the old episodes away and collect fresh ones. That is what makes plain REINFORCE so sample-hungry compared to off-policy value methods like DQN.

## 25.4 — Actor-critic and the advantage function

REINFORCE waits until the episode ends to know how good an action was, and weights it by the whole noisy return. **Actor-critic** methods fix both issues by learning two things at once: an **actor** $\pi_\theta(a\mid s)$ that picks actions, and a **critic** $V_\phi(s)$ that estimates how good states are. The critic provides the baseline and lets us bootstrap, so we no longer wait for the episode to finish.

The connective tissue is the **advantage function**, which asks: *how much better was this action than the policy's average behavior in this state?*

$$A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s)$$

A positive advantage means "this action beat expectations — make it more likely"; negative means "worse than expected — make it less likely." In practice you estimate it with the one-step TD error, which uses the critic as both the value and the bootstrap target:

$$\hat{A}_t = \underbrace{r_t + \gamma V_\phi(s_{t+1})}_{\text{bootstrapped target}} - V_\phi(s_t)$$

The policy gradient becomes $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t\mid s_t)\,\hat{A}_t$, and the critic is trained by regression to minimize $(r_t + \gamma V_\phi(s_{t+1}) - V_\phi(s_t))^2$. The two learn together: a better critic gives a lower-variance advantage signal, which gives a better actor.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    S[state s] --> A[Actor pi_theta]
    S --> C[Critic V_phi]
    A -->|action a| E[Environment]
    E -->|reward r, next state| C
    C -->|advantage A_hat| A
    C -->|TD error| C
```

**A2C** (Advantage Actor-Critic) is the clean synchronous version of this idea; **A3C** (Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic) ran many actor-learners in parallel on CPU, each exploring a different copy of the environment and asynchronously updating shared weights — decorrelating data through parallelism instead of a replay buffer. A practical middle ground between the one-step TD error (low variance, biased) and the full Monte Carlo return (unbiased, high variance) is **Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE)**, which blends $n$-step returns with a decay parameter $\lambda$ to tune the bias–variance tradeoff.

::: {.callout-tip}
The mental model: the actor proposes, the critic grades on a curve. Subtracting $V(s)$ means the actor is rewarded for *beating the baseline of this state*, not for landing in a state that was already good — which is what kills the variance.
:::

## 25.5 — PPO, TRPO, and the trust-region idea

Vanilla policy gradients have a treacherous failure mode: take one step that is too large and the policy collapses, because the data you collected is only valid *near* the policy that collected it. A bad step changes the policy so much that the next batch of data comes from a totally different (often worse) policy, and there is no recovery. The fix is to **limit how far the policy can move in a single update** — a *trust region*.

**TRPO** (Trust Region Policy Optimization) makes this precise: maximize the expected improvement subject to a hard constraint that the new policy stays within a **KL-divergence** ball of the old one, $\mathrm{KL}(\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}} \,\|\, \pi_\theta) \le \delta$. (KL divergence measures how much two probability distributions differ; bounding it keeps the new policy close to the old.) It works and gives monotonic improvement guarantees, but the constrained optimization (second-order, conjugate gradients) is heavy to implement.

**PPO** (Proximal Policy Optimization) is the lazy, brilliant simplification that became the workhorse of modern deep RL. Instead of a hard KL constraint, it bakes the "don't move too far" idea into the objective with a **clipped surrogate**. Define the probability ratio between new and old policies:

$$r_t(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t\mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t\mid s_t)}$$

Then optimize:

$$L^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_t\Big[\min\big(r_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t,\; \text{clip}(r_t(\theta),\, 1-\epsilon,\, 1+\epsilon)\,\hat{A}_t\big)\Big]$$

The clip (typically $\epsilon = 0.2$) is the whole trick. When the advantage is positive, the objective stops rewarding you once $r_t$ exceeds $1+\epsilon$ — there is no incentive to push the action's probability arbitrarily high off one batch. When the advantage is negative, it stops once $r_t$ drops below $1-\epsilon$. The `min` ensures the clipping only ever removes incentive, never adds it, so the update stays conservative.

```python
# PPO clipped objective core
ratio = exp(logp_new - logp_old)          # r_t(theta), old logp is detached/stored
unclipped = ratio * adv                    # standard surrogate
clipped   = clip(ratio, 1-eps, 1+eps) * adv
loss_pi   = -minimum(unclipped, clipped).mean()   # pessimistic bound
# total loss usually adds value loss + entropy bonus:
loss = loss_pi + 0.5 * value_loss - 0.01 * entropy
```

PPO's appeal is that it is *first-order* (plain Adam works), reuses each batch for several epochs of minibatch updates, and is forgiving of hyperparameters. That robustness is why it became the default for continuous control and, crucially, for **RLHF** (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) — the alignment step that tunes large language models. There, the "environment" is the model generating text, the "reward" comes from a learned reward model of human preferences, and PPO keeps the fine-tuned policy from drifting too far from the base model (often with an explicit KL penalty back to the reference). See the chapters on prompting/fine-tuning/alignment and LLMs for that story.

::: {.callout-warning}
The clip alone does not strictly bound the KL divergence — PPO can still occasionally take large steps. Production implementations add safeguards (early stopping when KL exceeds a target, advantage normalization, careful learning-rate schedules). PPO's reputation for robustness comes as much from these implementation details as from the clipped objective itself.
:::

## 25.6 — Continuous control: DDPG, TD3, and SAC

DQN's $\max_{a'} Q(s',a')$ is a dealbreaker for continuous actions — you cannot enumerate a steering angle or a robot's joint torques. A family of **off-policy actor-critic** methods solves this by learning a deterministic or squashed-Gaussian actor that *outputs* the maximizing action directly, while a critic learns $Q(s,a)$.

**DDPG** (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) marries DQN's replay buffer and target networks with a deterministic actor $\mu_\theta(s)$. The critic learns $Q$ from the Bellman target $r + \gamma Q(s', \mu(s'))$, and the actor is trained to output actions that maximize the critic — literally following $\nabla_a Q$ up the value surface. It is sample-efficient (off-policy, reuses replay) but notoriously brittle: it inherits and amplifies Q-learning's overestimation bias.

**TD3** (Twin Delayed DDPG) is DDPG with three targeted fixes for that brittleness:

- **Twin critics**: learn two $Q$ networks and use the *minimum* of the two in the target, biasing toward underestimation to counter the overestimation that wrecks DDPG.
- **Delayed policy updates**: update the actor (and targets) less often than the critics, so the policy chases a more settled value estimate.
- **Target policy smoothing**: add clipped noise to the target action, so the critic cannot exploit sharp, spurious peaks in $Q$.

**SAC** (Soft Actor-Critic) takes a different and now-dominant tack: **maximum-entropy RL**. It augments the reward with an entropy bonus, so the objective is to maximize return *and* keep acting as randomly as possible while still doing well:

$$J(\pi) = \sum_t \mathbb{E}\big[r_t + \alpha\, \mathcal{H}(\pi(\cdot\mid s_t))\big]$$

The entropy term $\mathcal{H}$ (a measure of how spread-out the action distribution is, scaled by temperature $\alpha$) bakes exploration directly into the objective and makes SAC remarkably stable and sample-efficient. It uses a stochastic squashed-Gaussian actor, twin critics like TD3, and typically auto-tunes $\alpha$. For real-robot and simulated continuous-control benchmarks, SAC is often the default choice.

| Algorithm | Actions | On/off-policy | Sample efficiency | Stability | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DQN (+Rainbow) | discrete | off-policy | medium | medium | Atari, discrete games |
| REINFORCE | any | on-policy | low | low | teaching, small problems |
| A2C / A3C | any | on-policy | low–medium | medium | parallelized baselines |
| PPO | any | on-policy | medium | high | the default; RLHF |
| DDPG | continuous | off-policy | high | low | continuous control (legacy) |
| TD3 | continuous | off-policy | high | medium–high | continuous control |
| SAC | continuous | off-policy | high | high | robotics, continuous control |

## 25.7 — Model-based RL and world models

Everything so far is **model-free**: the agent learns values or policies directly from trial and error, never building an explicit understanding of how the world works. That is simple and general but *sample-hungry* — millions of interactions for tasks a human grasps in minutes. **Model-based RL** trades that off by learning a model of the environment's dynamics, $\hat{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ (where the world goes next) and $\hat{r}(s,a)$ (what reward you get), and then *using* it.

Once you have a learned dynamics model you can do two powerful things: **plan** by searching or rolling out imagined trajectories before acting, and **generate synthetic experience** to train a policy without touching the real (expensive, slow, or dangerous) environment. The payoff is dramatically better sample efficiency; the risk is **model bias** — the policy learns to exploit errors in an imperfect model, behaving brilliantly in imagination and badly in reality.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    R[Real environment] -->|transitions| M[Learn dynamics model P_hat, r_hat]
    M -->|imagined rollouts| P[Plan / train policy in imagination]
    P -->|policy| R
```

**World models** are the deep-learning incarnation of this idea: train a network (often a latent-dynamics model with a learned compact state) to predict future observations, then learn the policy almost entirely *inside* that learned latent simulator. The **Dreamer** line of work is the prominent example — it learns a recurrent latent world model and trains an actor-critic purely on imagined latent rollouts, reaching strong performance with far fewer real-environment steps than model-free methods. The tradeoff remains the central tension of model-based RL: a more accurate model buys sample efficiency, but every modeling error is a crack the optimizer will pry open.

## 25.8 — Landmark systems: AlphaGo, AlphaZero, MuZero

The clearest demonstration of deep RL's power is DeepMind's game-playing lineage, which is worth understanding as a progression of *removing assumptions*.

**AlphaGo** (2016) beat world champion Lee Sedol at Go, a game long considered out of reach for computers because of its astronomical branching factor. It combined three ingredients: deep convolutional networks for a **policy** (which moves look promising) and a **value** (who is winning), **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)** to look ahead by selectively expanding the most promising lines, and bootstrapping from a database of human expert games before improving through self-play.

**AlphaZero** (2017) removed the human data entirely. Starting from random weights and knowing only the rules, it learned Go, chess, and shogi purely through **self-play**: MCTS guided by a single network produces strong move distributions, those games become training targets for the network, and the improved network makes MCTS stronger still — a virtuous loop with no human knowledge in the inner loop. It surpassed every prior engine.

**MuZero** (2019) removed the *rules* themselves. AlphaZero needed a perfect simulator to run MCTS; MuZero learns its own internal model of dynamics — but cleverly, it only models what matters for planning (predicted reward, value, and policy), not the full observation. It plans inside this learned latent model, achieving AlphaZero-level play in board games *and* mastering Atari from pixels, where no rules-based simulator exists. The arc — expert data → self-play → learned model — traces deep RL's trajectory toward generality.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    AG[AlphaGo: human data + MCTS + nets] --> AZ[AlphaZero: self-play, no human data]
    AZ --> MZ[MuZero: learned model, no rules]
```

These systems also surface deep RL's hardest open problems. They consume enormous compute and millions of self-play games — **sample efficiency** remains the field's central limitation outside of cheap simulators. Reward signals are often sparse and delayed (you win or lose at the *end* of a Go game), making **credit assignment** — figuring out which of the hundreds of earlier moves deserves blame or praise — hard. And **stability** is perpetually fragile: the data distribution shifts as the policy changes, targets bootstrap off the model's own estimates, and small changes in hyperparameters can be the difference between superhuman play and total collapse. Deep RL is powerful and, still, finicky.

## 25.x — Key takeaways

- Deep RL = RL with neural networks as function approximators, which is what lets agents handle huge or continuous state spaces — at the cost of severe instability (correlated data, moving targets, the deadly triad).
- **DQN** scaled value-based RL to pixels via **experience replay** (decorrelate, reuse data) and a **target network** (freeze the bootstrap target); it is off-policy and discrete-action only.
- **Policy gradients** (REINFORCE) optimize the policy directly via the **log-likelihood trick** — push up good actions, down bad ones — but are on-policy and suffer high variance, fixed by a **baseline**.
- **Actor-critic** learns a policy and a value function together; the **advantage** $A=Q-V$ ("how much better than average?") is the low-variance learning signal.
- **PPO** is the modern workhorse: a **clipped objective** that keeps each update inside a trust region with only first-order optimization; it also powers **RLHF** for LLM alignment.
- For **continuous control**, use off-policy actor-critics: **DDPG** → **TD3** (twin critics, delayed updates, target smoothing) → **SAC** (maximum-entropy, very stable).
- **Model-based RL** and **world models** (Dreamer) learn the dynamics to plan and gain sample efficiency, risking model-exploitation bias.
- **AlphaGo → AlphaZero → MuZero** trace a path from human data to pure self-play to a learned model; sample efficiency, credit assignment, and stability remain the open challenges.

## 25.y — See also

- **Reinforcement Learning Foundations** — MDPs, value functions, Bellman equations, the vocabulary this chapter builds on.
- **Model-Free Reinforcement Learning** — tabular Q-learning, SARSA, and TD methods that deep versions scale up.
- **Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory** — self-play and equilibria, extending the AlphaZero idea to many agents.
- **Neural Network Fundamentals** and **Training Deep Neural Networks** — the function approximators and optimization machinery underneath all of deep RL.
- **Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment** — RLHF, where PPO meets large language models.
- **Large Language Models** — the policies being aligned, and the systems MuZero-style planning is increasingly paired with.
- **Search & Problem Solving** and **Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing** — MCTS and the classical planning that AlphaGo and MuZero fuse with learning.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 24 · 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning](ch24.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [26 · ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory →](ch26.html)
